title: Content Moderation Pipelines
concept: Production generative systems run automated filters on both prompts and outputs to catch policy-violating or unsafe content before it reaches users.

exercises.prompt: Why do AI apps use automated filters on their inputs and outputs?

options.text: To make the AI run faster
To block harmful or unsafe text
To save storage space on servers

exercises.explain: Modern AI systems use safety filters to review and block harmful or inappropriate content before showing it to users.

cards.title: Double-Checking the AI
cards.body: AI models can sometimes generate weird, unsafe, or inappropriate responses. To keep users safe, modern AI apps don't just let the AI talk freely. They use an invisible safety pipeline to check everything coming in and going out!

cards.title: Content Moderation Pipelines
cards.body: A content moderation pipeline is a set of automated filters that scan both the incoming prompt and the generated output. It acts as a safety shield to block policy-violating, offensive, or unsafe text before it ever reaches the user.

cards.title: The Theme Park Security Guard
cards.body: Think of a content moderation pipeline like a security team at an amusement park. They check visitors' bags at the entrance gate (checking the prompt) to stop dangerous items from getting in. Then, they watch the park exits (checking the output) to make sure no one accidentally leaves with park property.

cards.title: Moderation in Action
cards.body: If a user asks an AI: 'How do I build a dangerous device?', the input filter catches the bad word and blocks it immediately. If a user asks a normal question but the AI accidentally generates a harmful response, the output filter catches it and replaces it with: 'I cannot answer that.'

cards.title: Myth: AI is Always Safe
cards.body: Many people believe that AIs are naturally polite and never need safety checks. In reality, AIs learn from the internet and can easily output harmful text. Automated pipelines are absolutely necessary to filter out bad content in real-time.

cards.title: Two-Way Checking
cards.body: A moderation pipeline checks data at the entry point (prompt) and the exit point (output) to keep the app safe.

right.text: Scans AI response for harmful words.

left.text: Scans user prompt for bad intent.

exercises.prompt: What is the main goal of using a content moderation pipeline in an AI application?

options.text: To make the AI answer questions much faster
To block unsafe, hateful, or policy-violating content
To translate the user's prompt into other languages
To store the user's personal password securely

exercises.explain: Moderation pipelines are designed to catch and block unsafe, harmful, or policy-violating content.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Content moderation pipelines only check the user's prompt, never the AI's final response.
exercises.explain: Safety pipelines check both the prompt sent by the user AND the response generated by the AI to ensure total safety.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the description of AI safety filters.
exercises.sentence: To keep users safe, a content moderation [blank] checks both what you type and what the AI says.
exercises.explain: An automated pipeline acts like a shield, screening inputs and outputs to ensure safety policies are met.

exercises.prompt: Which of these AI outputs was likely triggered by a content moderation safety filter?

options.text: AI: Here is a list of recipes for making chocolate chip cookies.
AI: I cannot fulfill this request as it violates safety guidelines.
AI: The weather in Paris is currently sunny and 22 degrees.

exercises.explain: Response B shows the safety pipeline in action, blocking a potentially harmful output and replacing it with a safe, standard refusal.

exercises.prompt: An AI chatbot app uses a content moderation pipeline. Why is it designed to screen data at two different stages instead of just one?

options.text: It checks both the input prompt and the generated output.
It only checks the input prompt before the AI sees it.
It only checks the output text after the AI generates it.
It bypasses checking and only monitors user feedback later.

exercises.explain: Excellent job! Content moderation pipelines are two-sided shields. They screen both the input prompt to prevent unsafe requests, and the generated output to ensure no harmful content is sent back to the user.
